conflicts.title: Herero & Nama Genocide
conflicts.description: The first genocide of the 20th century. German colonial forces under General Lothar von Trotha systematically exterminated the Herero and Nama peoples of German South West Africa (today Namibia), killing an estimated 65,000–80,000 Herero and 10,000 Nama. Survivors were driven into the desert and placed in concentration camps. Germany officially recognized it as genocide in 2021.

conflicts.title: World War I
conflicts.description: The Great War. Triggered by the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, it drew in most of the world's powers. The Allied Powers (France, UK, Russia, Italy, USA) fought the Central Powers (Germany, Austria-Hungary, Ottoman Empire). An estimated 20 million died. Led directly to the collapse of four empires and set the conditions for WW2.

events.title: Assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand
events.description: The killing in Sarajevo set off the chain of alliances that plunged Europe into war.

events.title: First Battle of the Marne
events.description: The Allied stand halted the German advance on Paris and began years of trench warfare.

events.title: Battle of Verdun
events.description: Ten months of attrition killed or wounded around 700,000 men for almost no ground.

events.title: Battle of the Somme
events.description: Over a million casualties; nearly 20,000 British troops died on the first day alone.

events.title: United States enters the war
events.description: American entry tipped the balance of manpower and materiel toward the Allies.

events.title: Armistice ends the fighting
events.description: The guns fell silent on the Western Front after four years and some 20 million dead.

conflicts.title: Armenian Genocide
conflicts.description: The Ottoman Empire systematically deported and massacred its Armenian, Assyrian, and Greek Christian minorities during and after WW1. An estimated 1–1.5 million Armenians were killed through mass shootings, death marches through the Syrian desert, and starvation. Turkey still officially denies the genocide.

events.title: Arrest of Armenian intellectuals
events.description: The round-up in the capital marks the start of the genocide, commemorated each 24 April.

events.title: Deportations and death marches begin
events.description: Armenians were driven on death marches into the Syrian desert; hundreds of thousands perished.

events.title: Genocide ends
events.description: An estimated 1 to 1.5 million Armenians had been killed; the population of Anatolia was destroyed.

conflicts.title: World War II
conflicts.description: The deadliest conflict in human history, killing 70–85 million people including 6 million Jews in the Holocaust. Nazi Germany under Hitler invaded Europe; Imperial Japan seized Asia-Pacific. The Allied Powers (UK, USA, USSR, France, China) defeated the Axis (Germany, Italy, Japan). Ended with atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki.

events.title: Germany invades Poland
events.description: The German invasion of Poland began the Second World War in Europe; Britain and France declared war two days later.

events.title: Germany invades France & the Low Countries
events.description: A rapid armoured offensive through the Ardennes overran France in six weeks and forced the Dunkirk evacuation.

events.title: Operation Barbarossa
events.description: The German invasion of the Soviet Union opened the deadliest theatre of the war, ultimately claiming tens of millions of lives.

events.title: Attack on Pearl Harbor
events.description: The surprise Japanese attack on the US Pacific Fleet brought the United States into the war.

events.title: Battle of Stalingrad begins
events.description: One of the bloodiest battles in history; the Soviet victory in early 1943 marked the turning point on the Eastern Front.

events.title: D-Day: Normandy landings
events.description: The Allied amphibious invasion of occupied France opened the Western Front in Europe.

events.title: V-E Day: Germany surrenders
events.description: Nazi Germany's unconditional surrender ended the war in Europe.

events.title: Atomic bombing of Hiroshima
events.description: The first use of a nuclear weapon in war killed an estimated 70,000–140,000 people; Nagasaki was bombed three days later.

events.title: V-J Day: Japan surrenders
events.description: Japan's formal surrender aboard the USS Missouri ended the Second World War.

conflicts.title: The Holocaust
conflicts.description: Nazi Germany's systematic state-sponsored murder of six million Jews and millions of others (Roma, disabled people, LGBTQ+, political prisoners, Slavs). Carried out through mass shootings, gas chambers at extermination camps (Auschwitz, Treblinka, Sobibor), and death marches. The most documented genocide in history.

events.title: Babi Yar massacre
events.description: Nearly 34,000 Jews were shot over two days as the Einsatzgruppen killings spread east.

events.title: Wannsee Conference
events.description: Senior officials coordinated the "Final Solution" — the industrialised murder of Europe’s Jews.

events.title: Warsaw Ghetto Uprising
events.description: Jewish fighters resisted deportation to the death camps for nearly a month.

events.title: Deportation of Hungarian Jews to Auschwitz
events.description: Around 430,000 were deported in eight weeks, most murdered on arrival.

events.title: Liberation of Auschwitz
events.description: Soviet troops entered the largest death camp; some six million Jews had been murdered.

conflicts.title: Nakba & 1948 Arab-Israeli War
conflicts.description: Following the declaration of the State of Israel, Arab armies from Egypt, Jordan, Syria, Iraq, and Lebanon attacked. The war resulted in Israel's establishment and the expulsion or flight of approximately 700,000 Palestinians — the Nakba ('catastrophe'). Palestinian villages were depopulated and destroyed; refugees were barred from returning.

conflicts.title: Korean War
conflicts.description: North Korea, backed by China and the USSR, invaded South Korea in June 1950. The UN, led by the USA, intervened. The war ended in an armistice (not a peace treaty) restoring roughly the original border. An estimated 3–5 million died. Korea remains technically at war.

events.title: North Korea invades the South
events.description: Northern forces swept south, nearly overrunning the peninsula within weeks.

events.title: Inchon landing
events.description: A bold amphibious assault reversed the war and retook Seoul.

events.title: China enters the war
events.description: Chinese intervention pushed UN forces back and locked the war into stalemate.

events.title: Armistice signed at Panmunjom
events.description: An armistice — never a peace treaty — froze the war along the 38th parallel.

conflicts.title: Vietnam War
conflicts.description: The communist North Vietnam, backed by China and USSR, fought to reunify Vietnam against South Vietnam and its primary ally the USA. The US deployed over 500,000 troops and dropped more bombs than in all of WW2. An estimated 3.5 million Vietnamese died (military and civilian). The US withdrew in 1973; Vietnam reunified under communist rule in 1975.

events.title: Gulf of Tonkin incident
events.description: A disputed naval clash was used to justify a major escalation of US involvement.

events.title: Tet Offensive
events.description: A vast coordinated assault shattered American confidence that the war was being won.

events.title: My Lai massacre
events.description: US soldiers murdered hundreds of unarmed villagers; its exposure galvanised anti-war opinion.

events.title: Paris Peace Accords
events.description: The accords ended direct US involvement, though the war between North and South continued.

events.title: Fall of Saigon
events.description: North Vietnamese tanks entered Saigon, ending the war and reunifying Vietnam.

conflicts.title: Guatemalan Civil War & Genocide
conflicts.description: The Guatemalan military, backed by the USA (CIA-backed 1954 coup installed the regime), conducted a systematic genocide against indigenous Mayan communities during the civil war. Over 200,000 people were killed, 45,000 disappeared, and 626 villages were destroyed. General Ríos Montt was convicted of genocide in 2013 (later overturned).

conflicts.title: Cambodian Genocide (Khmer Rouge)
conflicts.description: The Khmer Rouge under Pol Pot seized power and carried out one of the most extreme genocides of the 20th century, killing approximately 1.5–2 million people (25% of Cambodia's population) through executions, forced labor, starvation, and disease. Intellectuals, ethnic Vietnamese, Chinese, and Cham Muslims were especially targeted. Vietnam invaded and ended the regime in 1979.

events.title: Khmer Rouge capture Phnom Penh
events.description: The Khmer Rouge emptied the cities and began remaking society at gunpoint.

events.title: The Killing Fields
events.description: Execution, forced labour and starvation killed roughly 1.5–2 million people.

events.title: Vietnamese invasion topples the regime
events.description: Vietnam ousted the Khmer Rouge, ending the genocide.

conflicts.title: Soviet-Afghan War
conflicts.description: The USSR invaded Afghanistan to support the communist government against Mujahideen rebels. The USA, Saudi Arabia, and Pakistan funded and armed the rebels (Operation Cyclone) to bleed the Soviets. An estimated 1–2 million Afghans died; 5–6 million became refugees. The Soviet withdrawal helped trigger the USSR's collapse. The Mujahideen later became the Taliban and Al-Qaeda.

events.title: Soviet invasion of Afghanistan
events.description: Soviet forces intervened to prop up the communist government, sparking a decade of war.

events.title: CIA-supplied Stinger missiles reach the mujahideen
events.description: US and Pakistani backing turned the insurgency into a costly quagmire for Moscow.

events.title: Soviet withdrawal complete
events.description: The last Soviet troops left after around a million Afghan deaths.

conflicts.title: Iran-Iraq War
conflicts.description: Iraq under Saddam Hussein invaded Iran following the Iranian Revolution, fearing Shia Islamic revolution spreading. The war lasted 8 years with an estimated 500,000–1 million dead. Iraq used chemical weapons against Iranian forces and Kurdish civilians. The USA supported Iraq with intelligence and weapons. Ended with a UN-brokered ceasefire with no territorial changes.

conflicts.title: Gulf War
conflicts.description: Iraq invaded and annexed Kuwait in August 1990, citing disputed oil fields. A US-led coalition of 35 nations launched Operation Desert Storm, defeating Iraq in 100 hours of ground war. An estimated 100,000–200,000 Iraqi soldiers died plus civilian casualties. Iraq was expelled from Kuwait but Saddam Hussein remained in power.

events.title: Iraq invades Kuwait
events.description: Saddam Hussein's seizure of Kuwait triggered a US-led international coalition.

events.title: Operation Desert Storm
events.description: A massive air campaign opened the coalition war to expel Iraq from Kuwait.

events.title: Kuwait liberated, ceasefire
events.description: A 100-hour ground offensive ended the war; retreating forces set oil wells ablaze.

conflicts.title: Bosnian War & Srebrenica Massacre
conflicts.description: Following Yugoslavia's breakup, Bosnian Serb forces (backed by Serbia) conducted ethnic cleansing of Bosniak Muslims and Croats. The Srebrenica massacre in July 1995 saw the systematic execution of ~8,000 Bosniak men and boys — Europe's worst genocide since WW2. The UN had declared Srebrenica a 'safe area' but Dutch peacekeepers failed to prevent the massacre. The war ended with the Dayton Agreement.

events.title: Siege of Sarajevo begins
events.description: The longest siege of a capital in modern history lasted nearly four years.

events.title: Srebrenica massacre
events.description: Around 8,000 Bosniak men and boys were murdered in a UN "safe area" — ruled genocide.

events.title: NATO air campaign
events.description: Operation Deliberate Force pushed the parties toward negotiations.

events.title: Dayton Agreement
events.description: The accords ended the war and split Bosnia into two entities.

conflicts.title: Rwandan Genocide
conflicts.description: In 100 days, Hutu extremists massacred approximately 800,000–1,000,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu — about 70% of the Tutsi population. The killings were organized by the government, incited by radio broadcasts (Radio Mille Collines), and carried out largely with machetes by ordinary citizens. The UN and international community failed to intervene despite clear warnings. France has acknowledged complicity.

events.title: President Habyarimana's plane shot down
events.description: The assassination of the Rwandan president was the spark that unleashed the genocide within hours.

events.title: Genocide begins in Kigali
events.description: Roadblocks went up and the systematic killing of Tutsi and moderate Hutu began, coordinated by militias and radio.

events.title: Nyarubuye massacre
events.description: Thousands who had sought refuge at a church were killed over several days, one of many church massacres.

events.title: Murambi massacre
events.description: Around 45,000 people gathered at a technical school were massacred; the site is now a genocide memorial.

events.title: RPF captures Kigali
events.description: The advance of the Rwandan Patriotic Front brought the genocide to an end as the government collapsed.

events.title: Genocide ends
events.description: In about 100 days an estimated 500,000–800,000 people were killed.

conflicts.title: First & Second Congo Wars
conflicts.description: Africa's 'World War' involved nine African nations and dozens of armed groups fighting in DR Congo. Triggered by Rwandan Hutu génocidaires fleeing into eastern Congo. An estimated 5.4 million people died, mostly from disease and famine. The conflict was driven by vast mineral wealth (coltan, gold, diamonds). Left eastern Congo in permanent instability.

conflicts.title: Kosovo War & NATO Bombing
conflicts.description: Serbian forces under Milosevic conducted ethnic cleansing against Kosovo Albanians, displacing ~1 million people. NATO launched a 78-day bombing campaign against Yugoslavia without UN authorization. Serbia withdrew; Kosovo declared independence in 2008, recognized by 100+ countries but not by Serbia or Russia.

events.title: Račak massacre
events.description: The killing of Kosovar Albanian civilians galvanised international intervention.

events.title: NATO bombing campaign begins
events.description: NATO launched a 78-day air war against Yugoslavia without UN authorisation.

events.title: Serbian withdrawal ends the war
events.description: Serb forces withdrew and Kosovo came under UN administration.

conflicts.title: Darfur Genocide
conflicts.description: The Sudanese government and Arab Janjaweed militias (later reconstituted as the RSF) attacked Black African communities in Darfur, killing 200,000–400,000 and displacing millions. ICC issued an arrest warrant for President Omar al-Bashir for genocide. The conflict continues intermittently and has merged with the broader 2023 Sudan Civil War.

events.title: Rebellion and Janjaweed campaign begin
events.description: Government-backed militias waged a scorched-earth campaign against Darfuri civilians.

events.title: US declares the killings genocide
events.description: International recognition grew as hundreds of thousands died and millions were displaced.

events.title: ICC indicts President al-Bashir
events.description: The first ICC arrest warrant for a sitting head of state, for war crimes in Darfur.

conflicts.title: Iraq War (War on Terror)
conflicts.description: The USA and UK invaded Iraq claiming Saddam Hussein had weapons of mass destruction (WMD) — a claim later proven false. Saddam was toppled and executed. The invasion destabilized Iraq, fueled an insurgency, and created the conditions for ISIS. An estimated 500,000+ Iraqis died. The Chilcot Inquiry (UK) found the invasion was not justified.

events.title: US-led invasion begins
events.description: The invasion was launched on the false premise of weapons of mass destruction.

events.title: Fall of Baghdad
events.description: Baghdad fell and Saddam's statue was toppled; a long insurgency soon followed.

events.title: Abu Ghraib torture revealed
events.description: Photographs of prisoner abuse by US personnel caused global outrage.

events.title: US withdrawal
events.description: US forces left after nearly nine years and hundreds of thousands of deaths.

conflicts.title: Palestinian Occupation
conflicts.description: Since the 1967 Six-Day War, Israel has occupied the West Bank and Gaza Strip, home to ~5 million Palestinians. The occupation includes illegal settlements (700,000+ settlers), military checkpoints, land confiscation, collective punishment, and blockade of Gaza. The ICJ ruled in 2024 that the occupation is unlawful and must end. The USA consistently vetoes UN Security Council resolutions against Israel.

conflicts.title: Syrian Civil War
conflicts.description: The Assad government's brutal crackdown on Arab Spring protests evolved into a devastating civil war. Russia and Iran backed Assad; the USA, Turkey, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia backed various rebel factions. ISIS emerged and controlled large swaths of Syria and Iraq. Over 500,000 killed; 13+ million displaced — the world's largest refugee crisis. The Assad regime collapsed on 8 December 2024 when an HTS-led offensive captured Damascus; Bashar al-Assad fled to Russia. Ahmed al-Sharaa became interim president in January 2025.

events.title: Protests erupt
events.description: Arab Spring protests met with lethal force spiralled into civil war.

events.title: Ghouta chemical attack
events.description: A sarin attack on rebel-held suburbs killed over a thousand civilians.

events.title: ISIS declares a caliphate
events.description: The Islamic State seized vast territory across Syria and Iraq.

events.title: Fall of Aleppo
events.description: Russian-backed forces retook Aleppo after a devastating siege.

events.title: Assad regime falls
events.description: A lightning rebel offensive toppled the Assad government after 13 years of war.

conflicts.title: Russian Annexation of Crimea
conflicts.description: Following the Euromaidan revolution in Ukraine, Russia annexed Crimea in March 2014 and backed separatists in Donbas (Luhansk and Donetsk). Crimea had been part of Ukraine since 1954. The UN General Assembly voted 100-11 against the annexation. Crimea is internationally recognized as Ukraine but administered by Russia.

conflicts.title: Yemen War
conflicts.description: A Saudi Arabia-led coalition (backed by the USA and UK with weapons and intelligence) intervened against the Houthi movement in Yemen. The war has created one of the world's worst humanitarian catastrophes: 377,000+ deaths (mostly from famine and disease), 21 million in need of humanitarian aid. The Saudi coalition has blockaded ports and bombed hospitals, schools, and weddings. Iran backs the Houthis. A ceasefire in 2022 is fragile.

conflicts.title: Rohingya Genocide (Myanmar)
conflicts.description: Myanmar's military (Tatmadaw) launched a 'clearance operation' against the Rohingya Muslim minority in Rakhine State, involving mass killings, rape, and burning of villages. Over 700,000 Rohingya fled to Bangladesh in 2017 alone. The UN Fact-Finding Mission concluded the military carried out genocide, crimes against humanity, and war crimes. The ICJ case against Myanmar is ongoing.

conflicts.title: Tigray War
conflicts.description: Ethiopia's federal government and Eritrea fought against the Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF) in northern Ethiopia. The conflict involved mass atrocities, widespread sexual violence used as a weapon of war, and a deliberate blockade of humanitarian aid to Tigray causing famine. An estimated 300,000–500,000 died — one of the deadliest wars of the 21st century. A peace deal was signed in November 2022.

conflicts.title: Russia's Invasion of Ukraine
conflicts.description: Russia launched a full-scale invasion of Ukraine on February 24, 2022, the largest military attack in Europe since WW2. Russia aimed to capture Kyiv and replace the government. Ukraine, backed by NATO weapons and Western sanctions against Russia, repelled the initial assault. The war has caused 200,000+ military deaths, 10,000+ civilian deaths, and 8+ million Ukrainian refugees in Europe. Russia has committed war crimes including targeting of civilian infrastructure.

events.title: Russia launches full-scale invasion
events.description: Russian forces attacked Ukraine on multiple fronts, including a drive toward the capital that was repelled.

events.title: Siege of Mariupol
events.description: A near-three-month siege devastated the city, including the bombing of a theatre sheltering civilians.

events.title: Bucha massacre revealed
events.description: After Russian withdrawal from Kyiv oblast, hundreds of murdered civilians were found in Bucha.

events.title: Kharkiv counteroffensive
events.description: A rapid Ukrainian counteroffensive recaptured thousands of square kilometres in the northeast.

events.title: Liberation of Kherson
events.description: Ukraine retook the only regional capital Russia had captured, forcing a withdrawal across the Dnipro.

events.title: Ukrainian counteroffensive
events.description: A much-anticipated southern offensive made only limited gains against deep Russian defences.

conflicts.title: DRC / M23 Conflict
conflicts.description: The M23 rebel group, backed by Rwanda (which denies it), seized large parts of eastern DR Congo including Goma (Jan 2025) and pushed toward Uvira in South Kivu. Rwanda profits from Congolese minerals smuggled through its territory. A US-brokered DRC–Rwanda peace agreement was signed in Washington on 27 June 2025, but M23 (excluded from the talks) kept advancing; the US sanctioned the Rwandan military in March 2026 for violations. An estimated 7 million are internally displaced.

conflicts.title: Sudan Civil War (SAF vs RSF)
conflicts.description: War between Sudan's national army (SAF) and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) — the reconstituted Janjaweed militia. The UAE covertly funds and supplies the RSF with weapons. The RSF has committed mass atrocities, including the 2025 fall and massacres in El Fasher (Darfur). Sudan has the world's largest displacement crisis: ~12 million displaced. The army retook Khartoum in 2025 but the country is effectively partitioned. An estimated 150,000+ dead and famine declared in multiple regions.

conflicts.title: Gaza War
conflicts.description: Following Hamas's October 7 attack killing ~1,200 Israelis, Israel launched a devastating two-year military campaign on Gaza. Over 72,000 Palestinians were killed (majority women and children), 90%+ of Gaza's buildings destroyed, famine induced through blockade, and nearly all 2 million people displaced. The ICJ found plausible evidence of genocide; South Africa led the case against Israel. A ceasefire took effect 10 October 2025, but Israel retained control of ~50% of Gaza and over 1,000 Palestinians were killed in violations through mid-2026. The USA, UK, and Germany supplied weapons throughout.

events.title: Hamas-led attack on southern Israel
events.description: A cross-border assault killed about 1,200 people in Israel and took some 250 hostages, triggering the war.

events.title: Israeli ground invasion of Gaza begins
events.description: Following weeks of airstrikes, Israeli ground forces entered the Gaza Strip.

events.title: First truce & hostage–prisoner exchange
events.description: A week-long pause saw the release of hostages and Palestinian prisoners before fighting resumed.

events.title: Famine warning for northern Gaza
events.description: An IPC-backed assessment warned that famine was imminent amid mass displacement and a collapse of aid.

conflicts.title: Western Sahara Occupation
conflicts.description: Morocco invaded and annexed Western Sahara in 1975 following Spain's withdrawal. The Sahrawi people, represented by the Polisario Front (backed by Algeria), have been fighting for independence. The UN considers Western Sahara a non-self-governing territory; Morocco controls most of it. A promised UN referendum on self-determination has never been held.

conflicts.title: Kashmir Conflict
conflicts.description: Kashmir has been disputed between India, Pakistan, and China since the 1947 partition of British India. India administers Jammu & Kashmir; Pakistan administers Azad Kashmir and Gilgit-Baltistan; China administers Aksai Chin. Multiple wars have been fought (1947, 1965, 1971, 1999 Kargil). India revoked Jammu & Kashmir's special status in 2019. Both India and Pakistan have nuclear weapons.

conflicts.title: South China Sea Disputes
conflicts.description: China claims approximately 90% of the South China Sea via the 'nine-dash line,' overlapping with the claims of Vietnam, Philippines, Malaysia, Brunei, and Taiwan. China has built artificial islands and militarized them. The ICJ ruled China's claims have no legal basis (2016), which China rejected. The Philippines and Vietnam have faced maritime harassment. The US Navy conducts freedom of navigation operations.

conflicts.title: Nagorno-Karabakh War
conflicts.description: A disputed region between Armenia and Azerbaijan with an Armenian majority. Armenia controlled it after the 1990s war. Azerbaijan, backed by Turkey and Israeli weapons, launched a 44-day war in 2020 and recaptured most of the territory. In September 2023, Azerbaijan launched a military operation; 100,000 Armenians fled in 24 hours — effectively an ethnic cleansing. The region is now under Azerbaijani control.

events.title: Conflict erupts over the enclave
events.description: Competing claims to Nagorno-Karabakh ignited as the USSR unravelled.

events.title: Ceasefire ends the first war
events.description: Armenian forces held the enclave and surrounding districts under a frozen ceasefire.

events.title: Second Nagorno-Karabakh War
events.description: Azerbaijan retook much of the territory in a 44-day war.

events.title: Azerbaijani offensive; Armenian exodus
events.description: A one-day offensive dissolved the enclave; almost its entire Armenian population fled.

conflicts.title: Sahel Jihadist Insurgency
conflicts.description: Jihadist groups (Al-Qaeda-affiliated JNIM, ISIS-GS) have destabilized Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger. Military coups in all three countries expelled French forces; Russia's Wagner Group (now Africa Corps) filled the vacuum. Mass atrocities against civilians are committed by both jihadists and military forces. An estimated 10,000+ die per year; millions displaced. The conflict is spreading to coastal West Africa (Togo, Benin, Ghana).

conflicts.title: Algerian War of Independence
conflicts.description: France's brutal counterinsurgency war to hold onto its colony Algeria. The French military tortured prisoners systematically, massacred civilians (Sétif 1945 killed up to 45,000), and used napalm. Over 1 million Algerians died. France only acknowledged the use of torture in 2018. Algeria gained independence in 1962 after 132 years of French colonial rule.

events.title: FLN launches the war
events.description: Coordinated attacks (the "Toussaint Rouge") opened the independence war.

events.title: Battle of Algiers
events.description: French forces broke the FLN network in the capital using systematic torture.

events.title: Évian Accords / independence
events.description: After some 400,000–1.5 million deaths, Algeria won independence.

conflicts.title: France's Role in Rwandan Genocide
conflicts.description: France armed and trained the Rwandan Hutu government and military (FAR) before and during the 1994 genocide. Operation Turquoise (June 1994) sent French troops to Rwanda but protected Hutu génocidaires from advancing Tutsi forces rather than stopping the killing. A 2021 French inquiry found France bore 'heavy and overwhelming' responsibility. Many perpetrators fled through French-protected zones.

conflicts.title: Françafrique — France's Post-Colonial Interference
conflicts.description: France's system of political, military, and economic control over its former African colonies (Gabon, Cameroon, Chad, Togo, Côte d'Ivoire, etc.). Includes backing coups, assassinating inconvenient leaders (Sylvanus Olympio of Togo, 1963), propping up corrupt dictators (Omar Bongo, Idriss Déby), and using the CFA franc to keep African economies dependent on France. French military has intervened militarily in Africa 50+ times since 1960.

conflicts.title: Kenya Mau Mau Uprising & British Repression
conflicts.description: The British colonial government in Kenya declared a state of emergency and systematically tortured, castrated, and killed thousands of Kikuyu people during the Mau Mau uprising. An estimated 1.5 million Kenyans were forced into concentration camps (called 'villagization'). The UK government destroyed incriminating documents. In 2013, the UK acknowledged the torture and paid £19.9 million in compensation to 5,228 survivors.

conflicts.title: British Mandate Palestine & Balfour Declaration
conflicts.description: The 1917 Balfour Declaration committed Britain to establishing a Jewish homeland in Palestine — without consulting the Palestinian Arab majority. During the Mandate (1920–1948), Britain facilitated Jewish immigration while suppressing Arab revolts (the 1936–39 Arab Revolt killed thousands). Britain ultimately handed the problem to the UN and withdrew, enabling the 1948 Nakba. The UK bears foundational responsibility for the Palestinian conflict.

conflicts.title: Nigerian Civil War / Biafra
conflicts.description: The Igbo-majority southeastern region of Nigeria declared independence as Biafra. The Nigerian federal government (backed by Britain and the USSR) blockaded Biafra, causing a famine that killed an estimated 1–3 million people, mostly children — shown in shocking photographs worldwide. The UK supplied weapons and diplomatic support to Nigeria throughout. France backed Biafra. The famine was used as a deliberate weapon of war.

conflicts.title: British Colonialism in Iraq & Chemical Weapons
conflicts.description: Britain occupied Mesopotamia (Iraq) after WW1 and drew the borders of the modern Iraqi state. Winston Churchill approved the use of chemical weapons against Kurdish and Arab rebels in 1920 ('I am strongly in favour of using poisoned gas against uncivilised tribes'). The RAF bombed civilians to suppress the 1920 revolt. Britain installed the Hashemite monarchy. The borders drawn by Britain directly caused subsequent conflicts.

conflicts.title: Congo Free State (Leopold II's Genocide)
conflicts.description: King Leopold II of Belgium ran the Congo as his private property (the 'Congo Free State'). His agents enslaved the population to harvest rubber, cutting off hands as punishment for failing quotas and killing anyone who resisted. An estimated 10 million Congolese died — half the population. This was one of the first mass atrocities documented by international journalism and human rights campaigns. Belgium only apologized in 2021.

conflicts.title: Transatlantic Slave Trade
conflicts.description: European powers — primarily Portugal, Britain, France, Spain, and the Netherlands — transported approximately 12.5 million enslaved Africans to the Americas. An estimated 1.8 million died during the Middle Passage alone. Slavery fueled the economic development of Europe and the Americas. Britain transported 3.1 million Africans (before abolishing the trade in 1807 while keeping slavery legal until 1833). Reparations campaigns continue today.

conflicts.title: Haiti's Independence Debt to France
conflicts.description: After Haiti became the first Black republic to win independence (1804), France forced Haiti to pay 150 million gold francs (equivalent to ~$21 billion today) as 'reparations' to former slave owners — or face military blockade. Haiti borrowed from French and US banks to pay this debt, which it finished paying only in 1947. This debt directly caused Haiti's poverty and underdevelopment. France has never apologized or offered restitution.

conflicts.title: Spanish Conquest of the Americas
conflicts.description: Spain's conquest of the Americas destroyed the Aztec, Inca, and Maya civilizations and devastated indigenous populations through massacre, enslavement (the encomienda system), and Old World diseases. The indigenous population of the Americas collapsed by an estimated 90% (tens of millions dead) within a century — one of the largest demographic catastrophes in history. Vast silver and gold wealth was extracted to Spain.

conflicts.title: French Colonization of Algeria
conflicts.description: France invaded Algeria in 1830 and ruled it for 132 years as a settler colony, seizing land for European colonists and killing an estimated hundreds of thousands to over a million Algerians during the conquest alone (some historians describe the early conquest as genocidal). Algerians were treated as colonial subjects without citizenship. The occupation only ended after the brutal 1954–1962 War of Independence.

conflicts.title: British Colonial Rule in India
conflicts.description: Britain ruled the Indian subcontinent (today India, Pakistan, Bangladesh) for nearly two centuries, first through the East India Company then the Crown. British policies caused repeated famines killing tens of millions, deindustrialized India's economy, and extracted an estimated $45 trillion in wealth (by some calculations). Independence in 1947 came with the catastrophic Partition.

conflicts.title: Genocide of Native Americans
conflicts.description: The United States systematically dispossessed, forcibly removed, and killed Native American peoples as it expanded westward. The Indian Removal Act (1830) drove the Trail of Tears; massacres (Sand Creek 1864, Wounded Knee 1890), broken treaties, deliberate destruction of the bison, and forced assimilation devastated indigenous nations. The pre-Columbian population of millions was reduced to around 250,000 by 1900.

conflicts.title: Genocide of Aboriginal Australians
conflicts.description: Britain colonized Australia from 1788 and, with the settler society that became modern Australia, killed the vast majority of the Aboriginal population through frontier massacres, introduced disease, and dispossession. The Aboriginal population fell from an estimated 750,000+ to around 75,000 by the 1930s. The 'Stolen Generations' — forced removal of Aboriginal children, continued by Australian governments into the 1970s. (In 1788 there was no 'Australia' — it was a British colony; Australia federated in 1901 and continued the policies.)

conflicts.title: Opium Wars
conflicts.description: Britain (joined by France in the second war) waged war on China to force it to accept British opium imports, which were addicting millions of Chinese. Britain seized Hong Kong and imposed the 'unequal treaties.' The wars began China's 'Century of Humiliation' and the looting and burning of the Old Summer Palace in Beijing (1860).

conflicts.title: Great Irish Famine
conflicts.description: During a potato blight, around one million Irish died of starvation and disease and two million emigrated, while British-ruled Ireland continued to export food to Britain throughout. British laissez-faire policy and indifference turned a crop failure into a catastrophe that cut Ireland's population by a quarter. Many historians argue British policy made it a man-made famine.

conflicts.title: Philippine–American War
conflicts.description: After buying the Philippines from Spain, the USA crushed the Filipino independence movement in a brutal colonial war. American forces used concentration camps, torture (the 'water cure'), and scorched-earth tactics. An estimated 200,000–1,000,000 Filipinos died, many from war-induced famine and disease. The USA ruled the Philippines until 1946.

conflicts.title: Maji Maji Rebellion
conflicts.description: When the people of German East Africa (today Tanzania) rose against forced cotton labour, Germany responded with a scorched-earth campaign, deliberately creating famine by destroying crops and villages. An estimated 200,000–300,000 Africans died, mostly from the engineered starvation. It was one of the deadliest anti-colonial repressions in African history.

conflicts.title: Italian Colonization & Genocide in Libya
conflicts.description: Italy invaded Ottoman Libya in 1911 and, under Mussolini, crushed Libyan resistance with concentration camps that killed an estimated 80,000–125,000 people (up to a quarter of Cyrenaica's population). Resistance leader Omar al-Mukhtar was executed in 1931. Italy used poison gas against civilians. The colony ended only with Italy's WW2 defeat.

conflicts.title: Russian Revolution & Civil War
conflicts.description: Following the 1917 Bolshevik Revolution, the Red Army fought the White movement and foreign interventionists. An estimated 7–12 million died, mostly civilians from famine, disease, and Red and White terror. Foreign powers (Britain, France, USA, Japan) intervened in support of the Whites. The war established the Soviet Union and Bolshevik dictatorship.

conflicts.title: Irish War of Independence
conflicts.description: Irish republicans fought a guerrilla war against British rule. Britain deployed the notorious 'Black and Tans,' who carried out reprisals against civilians. The war ended with the Anglo-Irish Treaty creating the Irish Free State, but partitioned the island — leaving Northern Ireland under British rule and sowing the seeds of the later Troubles.

conflicts.title: Rif War
conflicts.description: Berber Rif rebels under Abd el-Krim defeated Spain at Annual (1921) before Spain and France jointly crushed the short-lived Rif Republic. Spain and France dropped chemical weapons (mustard gas) on Moroccan civilians and villages — one of the first aerial chemical bombing campaigns against a civilian population.

conflicts.title: Holodomor (Ukrainian Famine-Genocide)
conflicts.description: Stalin's Soviet regime engineered a famine in Ukraine by forcibly confiscating grain and sealing borders to trap starving peasants, during the campaign against Ukrainian peasantry and national identity. An estimated 3.5–5 million Ukrainians starved to death. Many countries now recognize the Holodomor as a genocide; Russia continues to deny it.

conflicts.title: Stalin's Great Purge & Gulag
conflicts.description: Stalin's regime executed an estimated 700,000–1.2 million people and sent millions more to the Gulag labour camps during the Great Terror. Targets included political rivals, military officers, intellectuals, and entire ethnic groups. The broader Soviet repression and Gulag system killed millions over decades.

conflicts.title: Italian Invasion of Ethiopia
conflicts.description: Mussolini's Italy invaded Ethiopia, one of Africa's only independent states, using poison gas against soldiers and civilians and bombing Red Cross hospitals. The League of Nations failed to stop it. Italy's occupation included the 1937 Yekatit 12 massacre in Addis Ababa, which killed tens of thousands.

conflicts.title: Spanish Civil War
conflicts.description: Franco's Nationalist rebels, backed militarily by Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy, overthrew the elected Republic. Germany's bombing of Guernica became a symbol of terror against civilians. Around 500,000 died; Franco's subsequent dictatorship and 'White Terror' killed tens of thousands more. The Soviet Union backed the Republic.

events.title: Nationalist coup begins the war
events.description: Franco's revolt against the Republic split Spain and drew in foreign powers.

events.title: Bombing of Guernica
events.description: German and Italian aircraft razed the town, immortalised in Picasso’s painting.

events.title: Franco declares victory
events.description: The Republic fell, beginning a 36-year dictatorship.

conflicts.title: Second Sino-Japanese War & Nanjing Massacre
conflicts.description: Imperial Japan's invasion of China killed an estimated 15–20 million Chinese, mostly civilians. The Nanjing Massacre (1937–38) saw Japanese troops murder up to 300,000 people and commit mass rape. Japan's Unit 731 conducted lethal human biological-weapons experiments. Japan has never fully reckoned with these crimes.

events.title: Marco Polo Bridge Incident
events.description: A skirmish opened full-scale war between Japan and China.

events.title: Nanjing Massacre
events.description: Japanese troops murdered an estimated 200,000–300,000 and committed mass rape.

events.title: Japan surrenders
events.description: Eight years of war left China with 15–20 million dead.

conflicts.title: Bengal Famine of 1943
conflicts.description: An estimated 2–3 million people died in British-ruled Bengal as wartime policies diverted food and Winston Churchill's government refused to divert shipping for relief, instead continuing food exports. Churchill blamed Indians for 'breeding like rabbits.' Many historians consider it a man-made famine and a crime of the British Empire.

conflicts.title: Partition of India
conflicts.description: Britain's hasty 1947 partition of India into India and Pakistan triggered one of history's largest forced migrations — about 15 million people — and communal massacres between Hindus, Muslims, and Sikhs that killed an estimated 1–2 million. Mass rape and abduction of women were widespread. The hastily drawn Radcliffe Line also seeded the Kashmir conflict.

conflicts.title: Indonesian National Revolution
conflicts.description: After declaring independence in 1945, Indonesia fought a four-year war against the returning Dutch colonizers. The Netherlands conducted 'police actions' and committed atrocities including summary executions (the Rawagede and South Sulawesi massacres). International pressure forced the Dutch to recognize Indonesian independence in 1949.

conflicts.title: First Indochina War
conflicts.description: France fought to keep its colony in Indochina (Vietnam, Laos, Cambodia) against the Viet Minh independence movement. The USA funded up to 80% of France's war effort. France's defeat at Dien Bien Phu (1954) ended the colony and split Vietnam — setting the stage for the American Vietnam War.

events.title: War breaks out in Hanoi
events.description: The Viet Minh's independence struggle against France turned to open war.

events.title: Battle of Dien Bien Phu
events.description: The Viet Minh victory ended French rule in Indochina.

events.title: Geneva Accords partition Vietnam
events.description: Vietnam was split at the 17th parallel, setting the stage for the next war.

conflicts.title: Greek Civil War
conflicts.description: One of the first proxy conflicts of the Cold War: the Western-backed Greek government fought communist insurgents. Britain and then the USA (under the Truman Doctrine) armed and funded the government. The war killed around 150,000 and displaced hundreds of thousands, keeping Greece in the Western bloc.

conflicts.title: Chinese Annexation of Tibet
conflicts.description: China invaded and annexed Tibet in 1950. A 1959 uprising was crushed and the Dalai Lama fled into exile. Tens of thousands died in the conquest and subsequent famine; monasteries were destroyed during the Cultural Revolution. China has since suppressed Tibetan religion, language, and identity, and resettled Han Chinese — what Tibetans and rights groups call a cultural genocide.

conflicts.title: Suez Crisis
conflicts.description: After Egypt nationalized the Suez Canal, Britain, France, and Israel secretly colluded to invade. US and Soviet pressure forced a humiliating withdrawal, marking the end of Britain and France as first-rank global powers and a turning point in decolonization.

events.title: Nasser nationalises the Suez Canal
events.description: Egypt's seizure of the canal provoked a secret Anglo-French-Israeli plan.

events.title: Israel, Britain and France invade
events.description: The invasion succeeded militarily but became a diplomatic disaster.

events.title: Ceasefire under superpower pressure
events.description: US and Soviet pressure forced a humiliating withdrawal, marking the end of British imperial power.

conflicts.title: Hungarian Revolution
conflicts.description: When Hungarians rose up against Soviet domination and Stalinist rule, the USSR sent in tanks, crushing the revolution. Around 2,500 Hungarians were killed and 200,000 fled. The brutal repression exposed the reality of Soviet control over Eastern Europe.

conflicts.title: Great Leap Forward Famine
conflicts.description: Mao Zedong's forced collectivization and industrialization campaign caused the deadliest famine in human history. An estimated 15–55 million Chinese (commonly cited as ~30 million) starved to death while the state exported grain and denied the catastrophe. It stands as one of the largest man-made disasters ever.

conflicts.title: Congo Crisis & Lumumba's Assassination
conflicts.description: Soon after independence, Congo's first elected PM Patrice Lumumba was overthrown and assassinated in 1961 with Belgian and CIA involvement. The crisis ended with the US- and Belgian-backed dictator Mobutu seizing power, ruling kleptocratically for 32 years. It set the stage for decades of Congolese instability.

conflicts.title: Indonesian Mass Killings of 1965–66
conflicts.description: The Indonesian military under Suharto orchestrated the mass killing of an estimated 500,000–1,000,000 alleged communists, ethnic Chinese, and leftists. The US and UK provided lists of targets and diplomatic backing. The slaughter brought Suharto's 32-year US-aligned dictatorship to power.

conflicts.title: Chinese Cultural Revolution
conflicts.description: Mao's decade-long campaign to purge 'counter-revolutionaries' unleashed mass persecution, public humiliations, torture, and killings by Red Guards. An estimated 500,000–2 million people died, intellectuals were destroyed, and cultural heritage was obliterated across China.

conflicts.title: Six-Day War
conflicts.description: Israel launched a pre-emptive strike on Egypt, then defeated Egypt, Jordan, and Syria in six days, seizing the West Bank, Gaza, East Jerusalem, the Sinai, and the Golan Heights. This began the ongoing occupation of Palestinian territory and reshaped the Middle East.

events.title: Israel launches Operation Focus
events.description: Pre-emptive airstrikes destroyed most of Egypt's air force on the ground within hours.

events.title: Israel captures East Jerusalem & the West Bank
events.description: Israeli forces took the Old City of Jerusalem and the West Bank from Jordan.

events.title: Israel captures the Golan Heights
events.description: In the final days Israel seized the strategic Golan Heights from Syria.

events.title: Ceasefire ends the war
events.description: In six days Israel tripled the territory under its control, reshaping the region for decades.

conflicts.title: Bangladesh Genocide & Liberation War
conflicts.description: The Pakistani military launched Operation Searchlight against the Bengali population of East Pakistan, killing an estimated 300,000–3 million people and raping 200,000–400,000 women in a deliberate genocide. India intervened militarily, leading to the creation of independent Bangladesh. The US backed Pakistan despite its own diplomats' genocide warnings.

events.title: Operation Searchlight
events.description: The Pakistani army launched a brutal crackdown on Bengali nationalists and civilians.

events.title: Mass killings and refugee crisis
events.description: Up to 3 million were killed and around 10 million fled to India.

events.title: Pakistan surrenders; Bangladesh is born
events.description: Indian intervention ended the war and secured Bangladeshi independence.

conflicts.title: Yom Kippur War
conflicts.description: Egypt and Syria launched a surprise attack on Israel to recover territory lost in 1967. The USA airlifted arms to Israel while the USSR resupplied the Arab states, nearly triggering a superpower confrontation. The war led to the OPEC oil embargo and eventually the Camp David peace between Egypt and Israel.

events.title: Egypt and Syria launch a surprise attack
events.description: A coordinated assault on the Jewish holy day caught Israel off guard.

events.title: Israeli counteroffensive crosses the Suez
events.description: Israel encircled the Egyptian Third Army before superpower pressure halted the fighting.

events.title: Ceasefire
events.description: The war reset Middle East diplomacy and led toward the Camp David Accords.

conflicts.title: Chilean Coup & Pinochet Dictatorship
conflicts.description: The CIA backed the 1973 coup that overthrew Chile's elected socialist president Salvador Allende and installed General Pinochet. His dictatorship tortured tens of thousands and killed or disappeared over 3,000 political opponents. It became a laboratory for neoliberal economics imposed under terror.

conflicts.title: Argentine Dirty War
conflicts.description: Argentina's US-backed military junta 'disappeared' an estimated 30,000 people — leftists, students, journalists, and activists — through torture, death flights (drugging victims and dropping them into the sea), and the theft of their babies. The Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo still seek the missing.

conflicts.title: Operation Condor
conflicts.description: A US-backed campaign of political repression coordinated among South America's right-wing dictatorships (Chile, Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay, Paraguay, Bolivia). They shared intelligence and assassinated dissidents across borders, killing an estimated 60,000 people and disappearing tens of thousands more.

conflicts.title: Lebanese Civil War
conflicts.description: A 15-year sectarian civil war devastated Lebanon, killing an estimated 150,000. Syria and Israel both invaded and occupied parts of the country. Israel's 1982 invasion enabled the Sabra and Shatila massacre of Palestinian refugees by allied militias, killing up to 3,500 civilians.

conflicts.title: Angolan Civil War
conflicts.description: One of the Cold War's largest proxy wars. The Soviet- and Cuban-backed MPLA government fought the US- and apartheid-South-Africa-backed UNITA rebels. Around 500,000+ died over 27 years. Cuba sent tens of thousands of troops; the conflict became entangled with Namibia's independence and the fight against apartheid.

conflicts.title: Turkish Invasion of Cyprus
conflicts.description: Following a Greek-backed coup, Turkey invaded and occupied the northern third of Cyprus, displacing around 200,000 Greek Cypriots. Turkey still occupies the north as the self-declared 'Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus,' recognized by no country but Turkey. The island remains divided.

conflicts.title: The Troubles (Northern Ireland)
conflicts.description: A three-decade conflict over Northern Ireland's status between Irish republicans (mostly Catholic) and unionists (mostly Protestant), with the British Army deployed throughout. Around 3,500 died, including civilians killed by paramilitaries and by British forces (e.g. Bloody Sunday, 1972). The 1998 Good Friday Agreement largely ended it.

conflicts.title: Apartheid in South Africa
conflicts.description: South Africa's white-minority government enforced a brutal system of racial segregation and oppression against the Black majority for 46 years. It included forced removals, the Sharpeville (1960) and Soweto (1976) massacres, detention without trial, and torture. International boycotts and internal resistance led by the ANC eventually ended it; Nelson Mandela became president in 1994.

conflicts.title: Anfal Genocide (Iraqi Kurds)
conflicts.description: Saddam Hussein's regime carried out a genocidal campaign against Iraqi Kurds, killing an estimated 50,000–182,000. The 1988 Halabja chemical attack alone killed up to 5,000 civilians with poison gas. Western states, then aligned with Iraq against Iran, largely stayed silent.

conflicts.title: Sri Lankan Civil War
conflicts.description: A 26-year war between the Sinhalese-dominated government and the Tamil Tiger separatists (LTTE). The war ended in 2009 with the government's Mullivaikkal offensive, in which an estimated 40,000–70,000 Tamil civilians were killed in a few months — widely condemned as war crimes. Up to 100,000 died over the full conflict.

conflicts.title: Second Sudanese Civil War
conflicts.description: One of the deadliest wars since WW2: around 2 million people died, mostly southern Sudanese civilians from war-induced famine and disease, and 4 million were displaced. The war pitted the Arab-Muslim north against the largely Christian and animist south. It ended in 2005 and led to South Sudan's independence in 2011.

conflicts.title: Chechen Wars
conflicts.description: Russia fought two wars to crush Chechnya's bid for independence. The capital Grozny was flattened by bombardment — the UN called it the most destroyed city on Earth. An estimated 100,000–300,000 died, many of them civilians, with widespread atrocities, 'filtration camps,' and disappearances. Russia installed the Kadyrov dynasty to rule the republic.

conflicts.title: Somali Civil War & Al-Shabaab
conflicts.description: Somalia collapsed into civil war after 1991 and has had no stable central government since. The US intervention ended after 'Black Hawk Down' (1993). Ethiopia invaded in 2006; the jihadist group al-Shabaab continues a deadly insurgency. Repeated famines, most severely in 2011, have killed hundreds of thousands.

conflicts.title: Sierra Leone Civil War
conflicts.description: A brutal war funded by 'blood diamonds,' infamous for mass amputations of civilians' limbs and the widespread use of child soldiers by the RUF rebels. Around 50,000–70,000 died. Neighbouring Liberia's Charles Taylor armed the rebels in exchange for diamonds; he was later convicted of war crimes.

conflicts.title: Liberian Civil Wars
conflicts.description: Two civil wars killed an estimated 250,000 Liberians and used tens of thousands of child soldiers. Warlord Charles Taylor's rule and the regional spread of conflict devastated the country. Taylor was later convicted by an international court for crimes in Sierra Leone.

conflicts.title: Eritrean–Ethiopian War
conflicts.description: A devastating border war between the two Horn of Africa neighbours killed an estimated 70,000–100,000 over a barren strip of disputed territory. Tens of thousands of people were deported on both sides. The conflict froze into a tense stalemate until a 2018 peace deal.

conflicts.title: Algerian Civil War
conflicts.description: After the military cancelled elections an Islamist party was set to win, Algeria descended into a decade of civil war between the state and Islamist insurgents. An estimated 150,000–200,000 died, with massacres of entire villages and tens of thousands of disappearances — known as the 'Black Decade.'

conflicts.title: US War in Afghanistan
conflicts.description: After 9/11, the US and NATO invaded Afghanistan to topple the Taliban and hunt al-Qaeda. America's longest war (20 years) killed an estimated 176,000+ people, including 46,000+ civilians. Despite trillions spent, the Taliban retook the country within weeks of the chaotic 2021 US withdrawal.

conflicts.title: Russo-Georgian War
conflicts.description: A five-day war in which Russia invaded Georgia over the breakaway regions of South Ossetia and Abkhazia. Russia has occupied roughly 20% of Georgian territory ever since, recognizing the two regions as independent. It was a precursor to Russia's later interventions in Ukraine.

conflicts.title: Mexican Drug War
conflicts.description: Since Mexico militarized its fight against drug cartels in 2006, an estimated 350,000+ people have been killed and over 100,000 forcibly disappeared. Cartels, fueled by US drug demand and US-sourced weapons, control swaths of territory. The Ayotzinapa disappearance of 43 students (2014) symbolized state-cartel collusion.

conflicts.title: Libyan Civil War & NATO Intervention
conflicts.description: A NATO bombing campaign (led by France, the UK and the US) helped overthrow and kill Gaddafi in 2011. Libya then collapsed into a second civil war, open slave markets for migrants, and a proxy battleground: Turkey and Qatar backed the Tripoli government, while the UAE, Egypt and Russia (Wagner) backed General Haftar. The country remains split.

conflicts.title: South Sudan Civil War
conflicts.description: Just two years after independence, the world's newest country collapsed into civil war between factions loyal to President Kiir and VP Machar, along ethnic Dinka–Nuer lines. An estimated 400,000 died and millions were displaced, with famine and ethnic massacres. Uganda backed the government militarily.

conflicts.title: Boko Haram Insurgency
conflicts.description: The jihadist group Boko Haram (later split with an ISIS affiliate) has waged a brutal insurgency across the Lake Chad basin — Nigeria, Niger, Chad and Cameroon. It is infamous for the 2014 abduction of 276 Chibok schoolgirls and mass killings. The conflict has killed an estimated 350,000 (including indirect deaths) and displaced millions.

conflicts.title: ISIS Caliphate & Yazidi Genocide
conflicts.description: ISIS seized a third of Iraq and Syria and declared a 'caliphate,' committing genocide against the Yazidis (mass killings, and enslavement of thousands of women and girls), beheadings, and mass executions. A US-led coalition, along with Russia, Iran, and Kurdish forces, fought to destroy it; the territorial caliphate fell in 2019.

conflicts.title: Uyghur Genocide (Xinjiang)
conflicts.description: China has detained an estimated 1+ million Uyghurs and other Muslims in 'reeducation' camps in Xinjiang since around 2017, alongside forced labour, mass surveillance, forced sterilization, family separation, and the suppression of Uyghur culture and religion. Several governments and parliaments have formally declared it a genocide; China denies it.

conflicts.title: Syria After Assad (Transition)
conflicts.description: After the fall of Assad in December 2024, Syria's HTS-led transitional government under Ahmed al-Sharaa has struggled to stabilize the country. Sectarian massacres of Alawite and Druze civilians occurred in 2025, clashes with the Kurdish-led SDF escalated in Aleppo in January 2026, and Israel has carried out hundreds of airstrikes on Syrian military sites.

conflicts.title: Israel–Hezbollah War (Lebanon)
conflicts.description: Israel and Hezbollah escalated from cross-border exchanges (tied to the Gaza war) into full war in 2024, including Israel's pager-explosion attack, the killing of Hezbollah leader Hassan Nasrallah, and a ground invasion of southern Lebanon. A November 2024 ceasefire was repeatedly violated; renewed fighting and fresh US-brokered ceasefires followed in 2026. Iran backs Hezbollah.

conflicts.title: Israel–Iran War
conflicts.description: After the 12-Day War of June 2025, in which Israel struck Iran's nuclear and military infrastructure, open war broke out again from February 2026 when US and Israeli airstrikes killed Iranian officials and Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. The US joined Israel directly against Iran and its allies; after five weeks, a US–Iran ceasefire (including Israel) was reached in mid-June 2026.

conflicts.title: US Intervention in Venezuela & Capture of Maduro
conflicts.description: After months of a Caribbean military buildup and strikes on alleged drug-trafficking boats, the US launched 'Operation Absolute Resolve' on 3 January 2026, bombing northern Venezuela and capturing President Nicolás Maduro, who was flown to New York to face narcotrafficking charges. At least 23 Venezuelan and 32 Cuban security personnel were killed. The UN and many states condemned it as a violation of sovereignty and international law.

conflicts.title: Venezuelan Crisis & US Sanctions
conflicts.description: Venezuela's economy collapsed under Maduro amid hyperinflation, shortages, and authoritarian rule, driving over 7 million people to flee — one of the largest displacement crises in the world. The US imposed crushing sanctions (especially on oil) from 2017, which critics say deepened civilian suffering, while backing opposition claims to the presidency.

conflicts.title: US Embargo & Bay of Pigs (Cuba)
conflicts.description: After Cuba's 1959 revolution, the US imposed a comprehensive economic embargo (still in place after 60+ years) and backed the failed 1961 Bay of Pigs invasion by exiles. The 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis brought the world to the brink of nuclear war. The UN General Assembly has voted overwhelmingly against the embargo every year.

conflicts.title: Colombian Armed Conflict (FARC)
conflicts.description: A six-decade war between the Colombian state, leftist guerrillas (FARC, ELN), and right-wing paramilitaries, fueled by the cocaine trade. The US funded the government heavily through 'Plan Colombia.' Around 260,000 died and over 7 million were displaced before the 2016 peace deal with the FARC; the ELN conflict continues.

conflicts.title: Salvadoran Civil War
conflicts.description: A US-backed military government fought leftist FMLN guerrillas, killing around 75,000 — mostly civilians murdered by army death squads. Atrocities included the assassination of Archbishop Óscar Romero (1980) and the El Mozote massacre (1981), in which the US-trained Atlácatl Battalion killed around 1,000 villagers.

conflicts.title: Nicaraguan Contra War
conflicts.description: The US covertly armed and funded the 'Contra' rebels against Nicaragua's leftist Sandinista government, including through the illegal Iran-Contra scheme. The CIA mined Nicaragua's harbours; the World Court ruled against the US in 1986. Around 30,000 died.

conflicts.title: US Invasion of Panama
conflicts.description: The US invaded Panama to depose its former CIA-asset dictator Manuel Noriega on drug charges. Hundreds to possibly thousands of Panamanian civilians died, and the poor El Chorrillo neighbourhood was devastated. The UN General Assembly condemned the invasion as a violation of international law.

conflicts.title: US Occupation of Haiti
conflicts.description: The US militarily occupied Haiti for 19 years, seizing control of its finances, rewriting its constitution to allow foreign land ownership, and reinstating forced-labour ('corvée') road-building. US Marines killed thousands suppressing the Caco rebellion. It was part of a wider pattern of US interventions across the Caribbean.

conflicts.title: Paraguayan War (Triple Alliance)
conflicts.description: The deadliest interstate war in Latin American history. Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay crushed Paraguay, which lost an estimated 60–70% of its population (and up to 90% of its adult men) to combat, disease, and famine. Paraguay was left devastated and stripped of territory.

conflicts.title: War of the Pacific
conflicts.description: Chile defeated Peru and Bolivia in a war over nitrate-rich Atacama territory. Chile annexed land that left Bolivia permanently landlocked — a grievance Bolivia still raises today. It reshaped the borders and economies of the southern Andes.

conflicts.title: Mexican Revolution
conflicts.description: A decade of revolution and civil war that overthrew the Díaz dictatorship and reshaped Mexico, with figures like Zapata and Pancho Villa. An estimated 1–2 million people died. The US intervened militarily several times during the upheaval.

conflicts.title: Peru — Shining Path Conflict
conflicts.description: The Maoist Shining Path insurgency and the state's counterinsurgency killed an estimated 70,000 Peruvians, most of them indigenous Quechua-speaking peasants caught between both sides. A truth commission documented massacres and forced disappearances by both the guerrillas and the military.

conflicts.title: Brazilian Military Dictatorship
conflicts.description: A US-backed military coup overthrew Brazil's elected government, ushering in 21 years of dictatorship that tortured tens of thousands and killed or disappeared hundreds of opponents. It was a pillar of the regional Operation Condor network.

conflicts.title: Taiping Rebellion
conflicts.description: A massive civil war in Qing China led by a millenarian movement that established a rival kingdom. It is one of the deadliest conflicts in all of human history, with an estimated 20–30 million dead, mostly from famine and disease. Britain and France ultimately backed the Qing to crush it.

conflicts.title: First Sino-Japanese War
conflicts.description: Japan defeated Qing China, seizing Taiwan and dominance over Korea. The war announced Japan's arrival as an imperial power and accelerated the decline of imperial China, feeding into its 'Century of Humiliation.'

conflicts.title: Boxer Rebellion & Eight-Nation Intervention
conflicts.description: An anti-foreign, anti-Christian uprising in China was crushed by an Eight-Nation Alliance (Britain, France, Germany, Russia, Japan, the US, Italy, Austria-Hungary), which looted Beijing and imposed a crushing indemnity on China. It deepened foreign domination of China.

conflicts.title: Japanese Colonization of Korea
conflicts.description: Japan annexed and ruled Korea for 35 years, suppressing Korean language and culture, conscripting forced labour, and forcing up to 200,000 women (mostly Korean) into sexual slavery as wartime 'comfort women.' The legacy still poisons Japan–Korea relations.

conflicts.title: Chinese Civil War
conflicts.description: The war between Mao's Communists and Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalists (paused to fight Japan) killed several million. The Communist victory in 1949 founded the People's Republic of China; the Nationalists fled to Taiwan, creating the still-unresolved cross-strait division.

conflicts.title: Tiananmen Square Massacre
conflicts.description: The Chinese military crushed peaceful pro-democracy protests in Beijing, killing hundreds to possibly thousands of demonstrators. China censors all mention of it to this day. The 'Tank Man' image became a global symbol of defiance.

conflicts.title: Laotian Civil War (US Secret War)
conflicts.description: During the Vietnam War the US dropped over 2 million tons of bombs on Laos — making it the most heavily bombed country per capita in history. Vast unexploded ordnance still kills and maims Laotians decades later. The CIA ran a 'secret army' of Hmong fighters.

conflicts.title: Malayan Emergency
conflicts.description: Britain fought communist insurgents in colonial Malaya, pioneering counterinsurgency tactics including the forced resettlement of 500,000 people into guarded 'new villages.' British forces committed atrocities such as the Batang Kali massacre (1948).

conflicts.title: Myanmar Civil War (Post-Coup)
conflicts.description: After the military (Tatmadaw) seized power in a February 2021 coup, mass protests were met with lethal force, igniting a nationwide civil war. The junta has conducted airstrikes, mass killings, and village burnings; thousands are dead and millions displaced. Ethnic armed groups and a resistance movement control much of the country.

conflicts.title: 1953 Iranian Coup (Mossadegh)
conflicts.description: The CIA and British MI6 orchestrated a coup that overthrew Iran's elected PM Mohammad Mossadegh after he nationalized the British-controlled oil industry. They reinstalled the autocratic Shah, whose repressive rule fed the 1979 revolution and lasting Iranian distrust of the West.

conflicts.title: Iranian Revolution
conflicts.description: A mass revolution overthrew the US-backed Shah and established Ayatollah Khomeini's Islamic Republic. It reshaped the Middle East, triggered the US embassy hostage crisis, and made Iran and the US enduring adversaries.

conflicts.title: British Occupation of Egypt
conflicts.description: Britain occupied Egypt to secure the Suez Canal and its route to India, controlling the country for over 70 years. British forces suppressed nationalist uprisings (1919, 1952) until the 1952 revolution and the 1956 Suez Crisis ended British dominance.

conflicts.title: Mahdist War (Sudan)
conflicts.description: A Sudanese Islamic uprising created the Mahdist State, which Britain and Egypt eventually crushed. At Omdurman (1898), British machine guns killed around 10,000 Sudanese in hours. The reconquest opened Sudan to Anglo-Egyptian colonial rule.

conflicts.title: Hama Massacre (Syria)
conflicts.description: Hafez al-Assad's regime crushed a Muslim Brotherhood uprising in the Syrian city of Hama, killing an estimated 10,000–40,000 people and razing much of the old city. It was one of the deadliest single acts of state repression in the modern Middle East.

conflicts.title: First Intifada
conflicts.description: A Palestinian popular uprising against the Israeli occupation, marked by mass protests, strikes, and stone-throwing met with Israel's 'force, might and beatings' policy. Around 1,200 Palestinians and 160 Israelis died. It led to the Oslo peace process.

conflicts.title: Second Intifada
conflicts.description: A far more violent Palestinian uprising involving suicide bombings and heavy Israeli military operations. Around 3,000 Palestinians and 1,000 Israelis died. Israel built the West Bank separation barrier and reoccupied Palestinian cities.

conflicts.title: Egyptian Coup & Rabaa Massacre
conflicts.description: After a military coup ousted Egypt's first elected president Mohamed Morsi, security forces massacred at least 800–1,000 protesters in a single day at Cairo's Rabaa Square — one of the largest killings of demonstrators in modern history. It entrenched Sisi's authoritarian rule.

conflicts.title: Boer Wars
conflicts.description: Britain fought the Dutch-descended Boer republics for control of gold- and diamond-rich South Africa. Britain interned Boer and African civilians in concentration camps where around 28,000 Boers (mostly children) and many thousands of Africans died of disease and starvation — among the first modern concentration camps.

conflicts.title: Anglo-Zulu War
conflicts.description: Britain invaded the independent Zulu Kingdom in South Africa. Despite the Zulu victory at Isandlwana, Britain ultimately destroyed the kingdom and absorbed it — a classic episode of the 'Scramble for Africa.'

conflicts.title: Ethiopian Red Terror (Derg)
conflicts.description: The Marxist Derg junta under Mengistu unleashed a campaign of mass killing against opponents, killing an estimated 500,000. Bodies were left in the streets and families charged for the bullets. Soviet- and Cuban-backed, the regime also presided over the 1983–85 famine.

conflicts.title: Idi Amin's Uganda
conflicts.description: Ugandan dictator Idi Amin killed an estimated 300,000–500,000 people, expelled the country's Asian minority, and ruled by terror. Tanzania invaded in 1979 to overthrow him after he tried to annex Tanzanian territory.

conflicts.title: Lord's Resistance Army Insurgency
conflicts.description: Joseph Kony's LRA waged a brutal insurgency across Uganda, South Sudan, DR Congo, and CAR, abducting an estimated 60,000–100,000 children as soldiers and sex slaves and displacing millions. Kony remains at large; the ICC indicted him in 2005.

conflicts.title: Burundian Genocides
conflicts.description: Burundi suffered cycles of ethnic mass killing between Hutu and Tutsi: the 1972 genocide of Hutus (an estimated 100,000–300,000 dead) and the 1993 violence that killed around 50,000–100,000 and helped ignite the wider Great Lakes crisis alongside Rwanda.

conflicts.title: Mozambican Civil War
conflicts.description: After independence from Portugal, Mozambique's Soviet-aligned FRELIMO government fought the RENAMO rebels backed by white-ruled Rhodesia and apartheid South Africa. Around 1 million died, many from war-induced famine, and the country was littered with landmines.

conflicts.title: Central African Republic Civil War
conflicts.description: A civil war between the mainly Muslim Séléka and Christian anti-Balaka militias has driven sectarian massacres and displaced a quarter of the population. Russia's Wagner Group props up the government in exchange for mining rights.

conflicts.title: Ogaden War
conflicts.description: Somalia invaded Ethiopia's Somali-inhabited Ogaden region. In a dramatic Cold War switch, the USSR and Cuba backed Ethiopia, sending thousands of Cuban troops to repel Somalia. Tens of thousands died.

conflicts.title: Zimbabwe — Gukurahundi Massacres
conflicts.description: Robert Mugabe's North Korean-trained Fifth Brigade massacred an estimated 20,000 Ndebele civilians in Matabeleland to crush political rivals. Zimbabwe has never fully acknowledged the killings.

conflicts.title: Cameroon Anglophone Crisis
conflicts.description: Cameroon's English-speaking minority rose against marginalization by the Francophone government, escalating into an armed separatist conflict ('Ambazonia'). Government forces and separatists have both committed atrocities; thousands are dead and over a million displaced.

conflicts.title: Malagasy Uprising
conflicts.description: France brutally crushed a nationalist uprising in its colony of Madagascar, killing an estimated 30,000–100,000 Malagasy through massacres, torture, and 'death flights' in which prisoners were thrown from aircraft. It is one of the lesser-known atrocities of French colonialism.

conflicts.title: Crimean War
conflicts.description: Britain, France, and the Ottoman Empire fought Russia over influence in the declining Ottoman lands. Around 500,000 died, mostly from disease — a scandal that launched Florence Nightingale's nursing reforms. It checked Russian expansion toward the Mediterranean.

conflicts.title: Franco-Prussian War
conflicts.description: Prussia's decisive defeat of France unified the German states into the German Empire and seized Alsace-Lorraine. The humiliation seeded French revanchism that helped fuel World War I.

conflicts.title: Balkan Wars
conflicts.description: Two wars in which Balkan states drove the Ottoman Empire out of most of Europe, then fought each other over the spoils. Marked by ethnic cleansing and atrocities, they destabilized the region and were a direct prelude to World War I.

conflicts.title: Soviet Invasion of Czechoslovakia
conflicts.description: The USSR led a Warsaw Pact invasion to crush the 'Prague Spring' liberalization. Around 500,000 troops occupied Czechoslovakia, ending reform and reasserting Soviet control over Eastern Europe for another two decades.

conflicts.title: Croatian War of Independence
conflicts.description: As Yugoslavia broke up, Croatia fought Serb forces seeking to carve out Serb-controlled territory. The war featured ethnic cleansing, the destruction of Vukovar, and the shelling of Dubrovnik. Around 20,000 died before Croatia retook its territory in 1995.

conflicts.title: Tajikistani Civil War
conflicts.description: A civil war erupted in newly independent Tajikistan between the government and an opposition of democratic and Islamist forces. Around 50,000–100,000 died and over a million were displaced; Russia backed the government militarily.

conflicts.title: Transnistria War (Moldova)
conflicts.description: When Moldova became independent, the Russian-speaking Transnistria region broke away with the backing of Russia's 14th Army. Russia still stations troops there, propping up an unrecognized breakaway state — a 'frozen conflict' like those in Georgia and Ukraine.

conflicts.title: Indo-Pakistani War of 1965
conflicts.description: India and Pakistan fought a second war over Kashmir, the largest tank battles since WW2. Thousands died before a UN ceasefire and Soviet-brokered peace restored the pre-war lines, leaving Kashmir unresolved.

conflicts.title: Operation Blue Star & Anti-Sikh Pogrom
conflicts.description: India's army stormed the Golden Temple, Sikhism's holiest site, to flush out militants, killing hundreds. After PM Indira Gandhi's assassination by her Sikh bodyguards, anti-Sikh pogroms killed an estimated 3,000+ Sikhs in Delhi, often with police complicity.

conflicts.title: Nepalese Civil War
conflicts.description: A Maoist insurgency against Nepal's monarchy killed around 17,000. The war ended with a peace deal that abolished the 240-year-old monarchy in 2008 and turned Nepal into a federal republic.

conflicts.title: Anglo-Afghan Wars
conflicts.description: Britain invaded Afghanistan three times during the 'Great Game' rivalry with Russia. The catastrophic 1842 retreat from Kabul annihilated a British army. Afghanistan earned its reputation as the 'graveyard of empires,' resisting British control and winning full independence in 1919.

conflicts.title: New Zealand Land Wars
conflicts.description: British colonial forces fought the indigenous Māori over sovereignty and the mass confiscation of Māori land in breach of the Treaty of Waitangi. The wars and confiscations dispossessed Māori and entrenched British settler control of New Zealand.

conflicts.title: Sengoku Period (Japan's Warring States)
conflicts.description: Nearly 150 years of near-constant civil war between rival samurai warlords (daimyo) fractured Japan. It ended with the country's reunification under Tokugawa Ieyasu, who founded a shogunate that ruled for over 250 years.

conflicts.title: Eighty Years' War (Dutch Revolt)
conflicts.description: The Protestant Low Countries rebelled against the rule of Catholic Habsburg Spain. After eighty years of war, including the sack of Antwerp, the Dutch Republic won independence and became a global trading and colonial power.

conflicts.title: Thirty Years' War
conflicts.description: A catastrophic religious and dynastic war fought largely on German soil, drawing in Spain, France, Sweden, and Austria. An estimated 4.5–8 million died, and some German regions lost a third to half their population. The Peace of Westphalia (1648) founded the modern system of sovereign states.

conflicts.title: Qing Conquest of China
conflicts.description: The Manchu-led Qing overthrew the Ming dynasty and conquered China, with massacres such as the Yangzhou massacre (1645). Decades of war and upheaval killed an estimated 25 million. The Qing forced Han Chinese men to wear the queue hairstyle on pain of death.

conflicts.title: English Civil War
conflicts.description: War between Parliamentarians and Royalists ended with the execution of King Charles I (1649) and a brief republic under Oliver Cromwell. Proportionally one of Britain's deadliest conflicts, it reshaped the balance between crown and parliament.

conflicts.title: Cromwellian Conquest of Ireland
conflicts.description: Cromwell's brutal conquest of Ireland, including the massacres at Drogheda and Wexford, mass land confiscation, and famine, killed an estimated 15–40% of the Irish population. Thousands were shipped to the Caribbean as indentured labour.

conflicts.title: Ottoman Wars in Europe
conflicts.description: The Ottoman Empire pushed deep into Europe, twice besieging Vienna (1529, 1683). The failed 1683 siege, broken by a Polish-led relief, marked the high tide of Ottoman expansion and the start of its long retreat from Central Europe.

conflicts.title: Mughal Empire Expansion
conflicts.description: The Mughals conquered and ruled most of the Indian subcontinent, one of the wealthiest empires of its age. Expansion under Aurangzeb included long, bloody wars against the Marathas and others before the empire's slow decline opened the door to British colonization.

conflicts.title: War of the Spanish Succession
