conflicts.description: A Europe-wide war over who would inherit the Spanish throne, pitting France and Spain against an alliance of Britain, Austria, and the Dutch. Around 400,000+ died; Britain gained Gibraltar and rising naval dominance.

conflicts.title: Great Northern War
conflicts.description: A coalition led by Russia broke the dominance of the Swedish Empire in northern Europe. Russia's victory under Peter the Great made it a major European power and gave it its Baltic coast, where it founded St. Petersburg.

conflicts.title: Dzungar Genocide
conflicts.description: Qing China exterminated the Dzungar Mongols of Central Asia, killing an estimated 80% of the population (around 500,000–800,000) through massacre, famine, and disease. The Qianlong Emperor ordered the destruction; the emptied land was resettled by other peoples. It is one of the clearest genocides of the 18th century.

conflicts.title: Seven Years' War
conflicts.description: Often called the first true world war, fought across Europe, the Americas, India, and the seas. Britain and Prussia defeated France, Austria, and allies; around 1 million died. Britain seized Canada and dominance in India, while war debts helped trigger the American and French revolutions.

conflicts.title: British Conquest of India (Mysore & Maratha Wars)
conflicts.description: Through the Anglo-Mysore and Anglo-Maratha wars, the British East India Company defeated the major Indian powers and seized control of most of the subcontinent, paving the way for the British Raj.

conflicts.title: American Revolutionary War
conflicts.description: The thirteen American colonies won independence from Britain, aided decisively by France. It created the United States and inspired revolutionary movements worldwide, though it left slavery and the dispossession of Native Americans intact.

conflicts.title: French Revolution & Reign of Terror
conflicts.description: The revolution overthrew the French monarchy and proclaimed the Rights of Man, but descended into the Reign of Terror, in which tens of thousands were executed by guillotine. The brutal suppression of the Vendée uprising killed up to 170,000 and is sometimes called a genocide.

conflicts.title: Haitian Revolution
conflicts.description: The only successful large-scale slave revolt in history: enslaved Africans in the French colony of Saint-Domingue overthrew their masters and defeated Napoleon's army to found Haiti, the first Black republic. France later punished it with a crippling 'independence debt.'

conflicts.title: Napoleonic Wars
conflicts.description: Napoleon's France fought a series of coalitions across Europe, briefly dominating the continent before his catastrophic 1812 invasion of Russia and final defeat at Waterloo (1815). An estimated 3.5–6 million died. The wars redrew Europe and spread revolutionary ideas and nationalism.

conflicts.title: Russo-Turkish Wars
conflicts.description: A long series of wars in which an expanding Russia steadily pushed back the declining Ottoman Empire, gaining the Black Sea coast and Caucasus and backing Balkan independence movements. The conflicts fed the 'Eastern Question' that destabilized 19th-century Europe.

conflicts.title: Mexican–American War
conflicts.description: The US invaded Mexico and seized roughly half its territory — today's California, Texas, Arizona, New Mexico, and more. It fulfilled US 'Manifest Destiny' expansion and left a lasting grievance over the lost lands.

conflicts.title: Circassian Genocide
conflicts.description: As Russia completed its conquest of the Caucasus, it expelled and killed the Circassian people. An estimated 600,000–1.5 million died or were driven into the Ottoman Empire, with mass deaths during forced sea crossings. Russia repopulated the emptied lands.

conflicts.title: American Civil War
conflicts.description: The war between the Union and the secessionist Confederacy over slavery and states' rights killed an estimated 620,000–750,000 — the deadliest war in US history. The Union victory ended slavery in the United States.

conflicts.title: Taiwan Strait Tensions
conflicts.description: After losing the Chinese Civil War in 1949, the Nationalists fled to Taiwan, which has governed itself ever since while China (PRC) claims it as a breakaway province and vows 'reunification', by force if necessary. China stages large military drills and incursions; the US is Taiwan's main arms supplier and security backer under deliberate 'strategic ambiguity'. A Chinese invasion is considered one of the most dangerous potential flashpoints of the 21st century.

conflicts.title: Falklands War
conflicts.description: Argentina's military junta invaded the British-held Falkland Islands (Malvinas); Britain sent a naval task force 8,000 miles to retake them in a 10-week war that killed about 900. The defeat hastened the fall of Argentina's dictatorship. The islands' sovereignty remains disputed.

conflicts.title: Anglo-Ashanti Wars
conflicts.description: A series of wars over the 19th century in which Britain conquered the powerful Ashanti Empire in West Africa (today Ghana), looting Kumasi and exiling the Asantehene. It was part of the wider 'Scramble for Africa' and Britain's Gold Coast colony.

conflicts.title: Anglo-Burmese Wars
conflicts.description: Three wars through which Britain conquered and annexed all of Burma (Myanmar), exiling its last king and ruling it as a province of British India. The conquest extracted teak, oil, and rubies, and the borders it drew helped fuel Myanmar's later ethnic conflicts.

conflicts.title: Amritsar (Jallianwala Bagh) Massacre
conflicts.description: British troops under General Dyer opened fire without warning on thousands of unarmed Indians trapped in an enclosed garden in Amritsar, killing several hundred to ~1,000 and wounding over a thousand. The massacre became a turning point that galvanized the Indian independence movement.

conflicts.title: Cyprus Emergency (EOKA)
conflicts.description: Greek Cypriot EOKA guerrillas fought a campaign against British colonial rule seeking union with Greece. Britain used detention camps and hangings to suppress it. Cyprus gained independence in 1960 — but unresolved tensions led to the 1974 Turkish invasion and the island's division.

conflicts.title: Rhodesian Bush War
conflicts.description: After white-minority Rhodesia declared independence to preserve white rule, Black nationalist guerrillas (ZANU and ZAPU) fought a 15-year liberation war. Around 20,000+ died before majority rule and the creation of Zimbabwe in 1980 under Robert Mugabe.

conflicts.title: French Mandate of Syria & Lebanon
conflicts.description: After WW1, France took control of Syria and Lebanon, crushing the Arab Kingdom of Syria and suppressing the Great Syrian Revolt (1925–27) — including the aerial bombardment of Damascus. France's divide-and-rule policies and the borders it drew (carving out Lebanon) shaped the region's later sectarian conflicts.

conflicts.title: Cameroon — Bamileke War (Hidden War)
conflicts.description: As Cameroon moved to independence, France waged a brutal secret war against the UPC independence movement, especially among the Bamileke and Bassa peoples. French forces used napalm, mass killings, and severed heads on display; estimates of the dead range from tens of thousands to several hundred thousand. It remains one of the least-known atrocities of French decolonization.

conflicts.title: French Wars of Religion
conflicts.description: Decades of civil war between French Catholics and Protestant Huguenots killed an estimated 2–4 million. The St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre (1572) saw thousands of Huguenots slaughtered in Paris and beyond. The wars ended with the Edict of Nantes granting limited tolerance.

conflicts.title: Spanish–American War
conflicts.description: The US defeated Spain and seized its remaining empire — taking Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines, and making Cuba a protectorate. It marked the United States' emergence as an overseas imperial power and led directly to the brutal Philippine–American War.

conflicts.title: US Invasion of the Dominican Republic
conflicts.description: The US sent 42,000 troops to the Dominican Republic to crush a pro-democracy uprising trying to restore the elected president Juan Bosch, fearing 'another Cuba.' Thousands of Dominicans died. It was part of a long pattern of US interventions in the Caribbean.

conflicts.title: Greek War of Independence
conflicts.description: Greeks rose against three centuries of Ottoman rule. Both sides committed massacres (Chios, Tripolitsa); the Ottomans' atrocities drew European 'philhellene' sympathy, and Britain, France, and Russia intervened, destroying the Ottoman-Egyptian fleet at Navarino. Greece won independence in 1832 — the first nation to break from the Ottoman Empire.

conflicts.title: Ottoman–Safavid Wars
conflicts.description: More than a century of wars between the Sunni Ottoman Empire and Shia Safavid Persia over Mesopotamia and the Caucasus, sharpening the Sunni–Shia divide. The Treaty of Zuhab (1639) fixed a border between Turkey/Iraq and Iran that still roughly holds today.

conflicts.title: Portuguese Colonial War
conflicts.description: Portugal fought to keep its African colonies — Angola, Mozambique, and Guinea-Bissau — against independence movements for 13 years. Atrocities like the Wiriyamu massacre occurred. The unwinnable war exhausted Portugal and triggered the 1974 Carnation Revolution at home, which finally freed the colonies.

conflicts.title: Italian Colonization of Eritrea & Somalia
conflicts.description: Italy colonized Eritrea and Italian Somaliland from the 1880s, using forced labour and racial segregation, before briefly forming 'Italian East Africa' with occupied Ethiopia (1936). Italian rule ended with defeat by the Allies in WW2.

conflicts.title: Eritrean War of Independence
conflicts.description: Eritrea fought a 30-year war against Ethiopian annexation — one of Africa's longest wars. Ethiopia, backed first by the US then heavily by the USSR, used famine and bombing as weapons. An estimated 150,000+ died before Eritrea won independence in 1991 (formalized 1993).

conflicts.title: First Sudanese Civil War
conflicts.description: Even before independence, southern Sudan rose against domination by the Arab-Muslim north. The 17-year war killed an estimated 500,000, mostly civilians. A 1972 autonomy deal paused it — but its collapse reignited the even deadlier Second Sudanese Civil War in 1983.

conflicts.title: Sino-Indian War
conflicts.description: China and India fought a brief, sharp war over their disputed Himalayan border (Aksai Chin and Arunachal Pradesh). China won decisively. The border remains militarized and undefined, with deadly clashes as recently as 2020 (Galwan Valley).

conflicts.title: Sino-Vietnamese War
conflicts.description: China invaded northern Vietnam to 'punish' it for toppling China's ally the Khmer Rouge in Cambodia. Both sides claimed victory after a bloody month that killed tens of thousands; China withdrew, and border skirmishes continued through the 1980s.

conflicts.title: Kurdish–Turkish Conflict (PKK)
conflicts.description: The Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK) has waged an insurgency for Kurdish rights and autonomy against Turkey since 1984, killing an estimated 40,000+. Turkey has bombed Kurdish areas in Iraq and Syria. The Kurds — a stateless people of ~30 million split across Turkey, Iraq, Syria, and Iran — are among the largest ethnic groups without a state.

conflicts.title: West Papua Conflict
conflicts.description: Indonesia took over the former Dutch colony of West Papua via a widely-criticized 1969 'Act of Free Choice' (just 1,026 hand-picked voters). A low-level independence insurgency continues; rights groups estimate 100,000+ Papuans have died under Indonesian rule, with ongoing repression and restricted access for journalists.

conflicts.title: Aceh Conflict
conflicts.description: The Free Aceh Movement fought for independence from Indonesia for nearly 30 years, with an estimated 15,000+ killed amid army abuses. The devastating 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami, which killed ~170,000 in Aceh, paradoxically pushed both sides to a 2005 peace deal granting autonomy.

conflicts.title: Moro Conflict (Philippines)
conflicts.description: Muslim Moro groups fought for self-rule in the southern Philippines (Mindanao) for half a century, with an estimated 120,000+ killed. A 2014 peace deal created the autonomous Bangsamoro region (ratified 2019), though splinter jihadist groups remain active.

conflicts.title: Philippine Drug War
conflicts.description: President Rodrigo Duterte's 'war on drugs' encouraged police and vigilantes to kill suspected drug users and dealers without trial. Official figures cite ~6,000 killed; rights groups estimate 12,000–30,000. The ICC opened a crimes-against-humanity investigation, and Duterte was arrested on an ICC warrant in 2025.

conflicts.title: Haiti Gang Crisis
conflicts.description: After the 2021 assassination of President Moïse, heavily-armed gangs seized control of over 85% of Port-au-Prince. More than 11,000 people were killed between 2024 and late 2025 and 1.4 million displaced. A Kenyan-led multinational force (2024) failed to stem it and was replaced by a UN-backed Gang Suppression Force in 2026. The crisis is fueled by weapons trafficked from the US.

conflicts.title: Red Sea Crisis (Houthi Attacks)
conflicts.description: In solidarity with Gaza, Yemen's Houthis began attacking commercial ships in the Red Sea and Bab el-Mandeb, sinking vessels and forcing much global shipping to reroute around Africa. The US and UK launched repeated airstrikes on Yemen (Operations Prosperity Guardian / Rough Rider). Attacks paused with the 2025 Gaza ceasefire but the Houthis vowed to resume after the 2026 US–Israel strikes on Iran.

conflicts.title: Portuguese Colonization & Slavery in Brazil
conflicts.description: Portugal colonized Brazil and made it the largest slave society in the Americas: roughly 5 million enslaved Africans were shipped there — about 40% of the entire transatlantic slave trade. Indigenous peoples were enslaved, killed, and devastated by disease. Brazil was the last country in the Western Hemisphere to abolish slavery, in 1888.

conflicts.title: Expulsion of Jews & Muslims from Spain
conflicts.description: In 1492 Spain expelled its Jews (the Alhambra Decree) — up to 200,000 forced to convert or flee — and the Inquisition persecuted those who remained. Muslims were forcibly converted then expelled (the Morisco expulsion, 1609–1614 drove out ~300,000). It was one of the largest religious cleansings in European history.

conflicts.title: Túpac Amaru II Rebellion
conflicts.description: The largest indigenous uprising against Spanish rule in the Americas, led by Túpac Amaru II in Peru against colonial forced labour and tribute. Spain crushed it brutally — executing the leader by dismemberment in the plaza of Cusco — and an estimated 100,000 died. It became a lasting symbol of indigenous and anti-colonial resistance.

conflicts.title: Spanish American Wars of Independence
conflicts.description: Across two decades, most of Spanish America won independence from Spain in wars led by figures like Simón Bolívar and José de San Martín. The wars freed today's Mexico, Colombia, Venezuela, Peru, Bolivia, Chile, Argentina, and more, ending three centuries of Spanish colonial rule and reshaping the continent.

conflicts.title: Argentine Conquest of the Desert
conflicts.description: Argentina's military campaign to seize Patagonia from its indigenous peoples (Mapuche, Tehuelche, Ranquel) killed thousands, enslaved survivors, and shipped children to forced labour. It opened vast lands for European settlers and is widely described as a genocide that nearly erased Patagonia's native population. Chile waged a parallel 'Pacification of Araucanía'.

conflicts.title: Chaco War
conflicts.description: Bolivia and Paraguay fought the bloodiest South American war of the 20th century over the Gran Chaco region, wrongly believed to hold oil. Around 100,000 died, many from disease and thirst in the harsh terrain. Paraguay won most of the disputed land; both impoverished nations were devastated.

conflicts.title: Cuban Revolution
conflicts.description: Fidel Castro and Che Guevara's guerrilla movement overthrew the US-backed dictator Batista in 1959, establishing a communist state 90 miles from Florida. It triggered the US embargo, the Bay of Pigs, and the 1962 Missile Crisis, and made Cuba a Cold War flashpoint and exporter of revolution.

conflicts.title: Cristero War (Mexico)
conflicts.description: A Catholic uprising against the Mexican government's violent enforcement of anti-clerical, secularist laws. Around 90,000 died before a US-brokered truce. Persecution of Catholics continued for years afterward.

conflicts.title: Canada — Residential Schools & Indigenous Genocide
conflicts.description: For over a century Canada forcibly removed Indigenous children to church-run 'residential schools' to erase their culture, where many suffered abuse, malnutrition, and death; thousands of unmarked graves have since been found. A 2015 Truth and Reconciliation Commission called it 'cultural genocide'. Combined with earlier dispossession and disease, it devastated First Nations, Inuit, and Métis peoples.

conflicts.title: Winter War
conflicts.description: The Soviet Union invaded Finland in 1939; vastly outnumbered Finnish forces inflicted huge losses in the snow before being forced to cede territory. The Soviets' poor performance encouraged Hitler to later invade the USSR. Finland kept its independence.

conflicts.title: Katyn Massacre
conflicts.description: The Soviet secret police executed around 22,000 Polish military officers, police, and intellectuals in the Katyn forest and elsewhere, aiming to decapitate Poland's leadership class. The USSR blamed the Nazis and denied it for 50 years, only admitting responsibility in 1990.

conflicts.title: Soviet Deportation of Nations
conflicts.description: Stalin forcibly deported entire ethnic groups — Crimean Tatars, Chechens, Ingush, Volga Germans, Kalmyks, and others — to Central Asia and Siberia, accusing them of collective disloyalty. Packed into cattle trains, an estimated quarter to nearly half of some peoples died of cold, hunger, and disease. The Crimean Tatar deportation is recognized by some as genocide.

conflicts.title: Kazakh Famine (Asharshylyk)
conflicts.description: Stalin's forced collectivization and seizure of livestock caused a catastrophic famine in Kazakhstan that killed an estimated 1.5 million Kazakhs — around 40% of the entire Kazakh population, the highest death ratio of any group in the Soviet Union. The traditionally nomadic Kazakhs were made a minority in their own land. Many call it a genocide.

conflicts.title: Mongolian Stalinist Purges
conflicts.description: Under Soviet direction, Mongolia's leader Khorloogiin Choibalsan purged 'enemies of the people' — executing an estimated 30,000–35,000, a huge share of a small population. Buddhist monasteries were destroyed and tens of thousands of monks killed, all but erasing organized Buddhism in Mongolia for decades.

conflicts.title: Basmachi Revolt (Central Asia)
conflicts.description: A long Muslim uprising across Central Asia against Russian Imperial then Soviet rule, sparked by the 1916 conscription revolt. The Red Army crushed it over nearly two decades, killing an estimated hundreds of thousands and consolidating Soviet control over today's Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan, and Turkmenistan.

conflicts.title: Black January (Azerbaijan)
conflicts.description: Soviet troops stormed Baku in January 1990, killing around 130+ civilians to crush Azerbaijan's growing independence movement. It shattered any remaining Azerbaijani loyalty to Moscow and is commemorated as a national day of mourning.

conflicts.title: Andijan Massacre (Uzbekistan)
conflicts.description: Uzbek government forces opened fire on a large crowd of mostly peaceful protesters in Andijan, killing an estimated several hundred to ~1,500. The regime of Islam Karimov sealed off the city and denied wrongdoing. It stands as one of the worst state massacres in post-Soviet Central Asia.

conflicts.title: Kyrgyz–Uzbek Ethnic Clashes (Osh)
conflicts.description: Ethnic violence between Kyrgyz and the Uzbek minority erupted in southern Kyrgyzstan (Osh and Jalal-Abad) after a political upheaval, killing around 400–2,000 and displacing hundreds of thousands. It echoed an earlier round of Osh clashes in 1990.

conflicts.title: Georgian Civil Wars (Abkhazia & South Ossetia)
conflicts.description: As the USSR collapsed, the breakaway regions of Abkhazia and South Ossetia fought Georgia with Russian backing, driving out around 250,000 ethnic Georgians (ethnic cleansing in Abkhazia). The conflicts froze into Russian-backed statelets — the same fault lines that erupted again in the 2008 Russo-Georgian War.

conflicts.title: Great Turkish War (Siege of Vienna)
conflicts.description: The failed Ottoman siege of Vienna in 1683 turned into a sixteen-year war in which a Holy League led by Habsburg Austria and Poland-Lithuania pushed the Ottomans out of Hungary. The Treaty of Karlowitz (1699) marked the first major Ottoman territorial loss and the turning point of Ottoman power in Europe.

conflicts.title: Khmelnytsky Uprising
conflicts.description: A Cossack revolt against Polish-Lithuanian rule in Ukraine that founded a Cossack state and led, in 1654, to alliance with Russia. The war was accompanied by large-scale massacres of Jewish communities and Polish nobility, with tens of thousands killed.

conflicts.title: King Philip's War
conflicts.description: An uprising of Wampanoag, Narragansett and allied peoples against the expanding New England colonies, and one of the deadliest wars in American history relative to population. It broke Native power in southern New England; thousands died and many survivors were sold into slavery.

conflicts.title: Shimabara Rebellion
conflicts.description: A revolt of overtaxed peasants, many of them Christian converts, in south-western Japan. The Tokugawa shogunate crushed it and executed tens of thousands, then sealed Japan off from the outside world and drove Christianity underground for two centuries.

conflicts.title: War of the Austrian Succession
conflicts.description: A pan-European war triggered by Prussia's seizure of Silesia and challenges to Maria Theresa's inheritance of the Habsburg lands. It drew in France, Spain, Britain and others across Europe and the colonies before the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle largely restored the pre-war map.

conflicts.title: Nader Shah's Invasion of India
conflicts.description: The Persian ruler Nader Shah invaded the Mughal Empire and sacked Delhi in 1739. His forces massacred an estimated 30,000 inhabitants in a single day and carried off the imperial treasury, including the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond, hastening the Mughal collapse.

conflicts.title: Partitions of Poland
conflicts.description: In three partitions (1772, 1793, 1795) Russia, Prussia and Austria progressively annexed the entire Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, erasing a large European state from the map for 123 years until 1918.

conflicts.title: Indian Rebellion of 1857 (Sepoy Mutiny)
conflicts.description: A major uprising against the rule of the British East India Company, sparked by mutinying sepoys and spreading across northern and central India. Its brutal suppression, with widespread reprisal killings of civilians, ended Company rule and brought India under the direct control of the British Crown.

conflicts.title: Java War (Diponegoro War)
conflicts.description: A five-year revolt led by Prince Diponegoro against Dutch colonial rule in Java. It was the costliest conflict of the Dutch East Indies: around 200,000 Javanese died, many from famine and disease, and it cemented Dutch control over the island.

conflicts.title: Mfecane
conflicts.description: A period of widespread upheaval, warfare and forced migration among southern African peoples, associated with the rise of the Zulu kingdom under Shaka. Estimates of the dead range widely, up to one to two million, and the disruption reshaped the demography of the region on the eve of European expansion.

conflicts.title: First Italo-Ethiopian War
conflicts.description: Italy's attempt to colonise Ethiopia ended in decisive defeat at the Battle of Adwa in 1896, where Emperor Menelik II's army routed the invaders. It was the first time an African state defeated a European colonial power and secured Ethiopian independence for another forty years.

conflicts.title: Caucasian War
conflicts.description: A decades-long campaign of Russian conquest across the Caucasus, subduing the mountain peoples of the North Caucasus and the South Caucasian states. It ended with the mass expulsion of Circassians and others, and folded the region into the Russian Empire.
